senses.definition: Libraries or literary clubs, especially ones that promote learning and the arts.
examples: The city once had several atheneums where writers and thinkers met.
Grants helped restore two historic atheneums in the old town.
Atheneums across the country are updating their collections to attract younger readers.

senses.definition: Relating to the city of Athens in Greece, especially in its ancient period.
examples: The museum has a famous collection of Athenian pottery.
Athenian democracy influenced many modern political systems.
She is studying Athenian philosophy at university.

senses.definition: A person from the city of Athens in Greece.
examples: The Athenians built impressive temples on the Acropolis.
As an Athenian, he is proud of his city’s long history.
Ancient Athenians placed a high value on public debate.

senses.definition: In a way that is characteristic of Athenians or of Athens, especially its culture or style.
examples: The building is decorated quite Athenianly, with columns and marble statues.
He argued Athenianly, emphasizing reason and public debate.
The festival was organized Athenianly, with theater, music, and open discussion.

senses.definition: People from the city of Athens in Greece.
examples: The Athenians defeated the Persians at the Battle of Marathon.
Modern Athenians face many of the same problems as people in other large cities.
Ancient Athenians are known for developing early forms of democracy.

senses.definition: A type of furnace used historically in alchemy for keeping substances at a steady heat for a long time.
examples: The alchemist carefully placed the vessel inside the athenor.
Old texts describe the athenor as essential for slow chemical changes.
In the illustration, the athenor stands at the center of the laboratory.

senses.definition: The capital city of Greece, famous for its ancient history and culture.
examples: They spent a week exploring the ruins in Athens.
Athens attracts millions of tourists every year.
The Acropolis is one of the most famous sights in Athens.

senses.definition: Not based on theology, or actively opposed to theological ideas.
examples: He develops an atheological critique of traditional religion.
The movement takes an atheological position on questions about God.
Her writing is boldly atheological and challenges church doctrine.

senses.definition: In a way that is not based on theology or that opposes theological ideas.
examples: The author writes atheologically, refusing to use religious explanations.
They approached the topic atheologically rather than from within any faith.
The discussion was carried out atheologically, focusing on human experience.

senses.definition: Systems of thinking or writing that reject or criticize theological ideas, or that study religion from a non‑theistic viewpoint.
examples: Modern atheologies often focus on the social effects of belief.
She compares different atheologies that oppose classical Christianity.
Contemporary atheologies explore morality without reference to God.

senses.definition: The critical study of religion or theology from a non‑theistic or anti‑theistic point of view.
examples: Her book is a major contribution to modern atheology.
Atheology examines religious claims without accepting belief in God.
The course covers both theology and atheology in equal depth.

senses.definition: Not based on any specific theory or not aiming to develop theory.
examples: The study takes an atheoretical approach and simply reports the data.
Many practitioners prefer atheoretical methods that focus on what works.
His criticism is mostly atheoretical and does not propose a new model.

senses.definition: Not believing in the existence of God or gods.
examples: Some writers in that period openly expressed atheous views.
He was condemned by the church as atheous and dangerous.
Her atheous outlook shaped her understanding of morality.

senses.definition: A former scientific name for a group of flies with certain antenna features, now usually treated under other taxonomic names.
examples: The genus was once placed in Athericera in older classifications.
Some nineteenth‑century books still refer to Athericera as a separate group.
Modern entomologists rarely use the term Athericera.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to Athericera, a historical group of flies.
examples: The fossil shows typical athericeran features.
Athericeran larvae develop in aquatic environments.
The museum displays several athericeran specimens.

senses.definition: A fly belonging to Athericera in older scientific classifications.
examples: The collection includes several athericerans from tropical regions.
Only one athericeran was found in the sample.
Early researchers described these insects as athericerans.

senses.definition: Having characteristics typical of Athericera flies, especially in the antennae.
examples: The specimen has athericerous antennae.
These insects show athericerous traits in their head structure.
The fossilized fly is clearly athericerous.

senses.definition: Relating to small silvery fishes of the silverside family Atherinidae.
examples: The atherine fishes are common in coastal waters.
He specializes in the study of atherine species.
Atherine forms can be found in both marine and freshwater habitats.

senses.definition: Small, usually silvery fishes belonging to the silverside group.
examples: Atherines often gather in large schools near the surface.
Fishermen reported catching many atherines close to the shore.
The lagoon is rich in tiny atherines.

senses.definition: A family of small, mainly marine fishes known as silversides.
examples: Atherinidae includes many species that live in coastal waters.
The scientist described a new genus in the family Atherinidae.
Members of Atherinidae are important in some local fisheries.

senses.definition: A former zoogeographical name for the combined regions of Africa and South America in some older classifications.
examples: The term Atheriogaea appears in early biogeographical literature.
In that system, Atheriogaea contrasted with the northern region Arctogaea.
Atheriogaea was proposed to group certain tropical faunas.

senses.definition: Relating to Atheriogaea, a historical zoogeographical region including Africa and South America.
examples: The species forms part of the atheriogaean fauna.
Early maps show an atheriogaean region separate from other zones.
Some plants have an atheriogaean distribution pattern.

senses.definition: A genus of small, often brightly colored African venomous snakes commonly called bush vipers.
examples: Atheris species live mainly in forested parts of Africa.
The herpetologist specializes in Atheris and related genera.
Several Atheris are popular among reptile enthusiasts, though they are dangerous.

senses.definition: Rare plural of athermancy, a supposed method of divination using heat or lack of heat.
examples: Old texts briefly mention athermancies among other strange arts.
He listed various athermancies but gave little detail.
Athermancies were never widely practiced.

senses.definition: An old, very rare term for a method of fortune‑telling using heat or changes in temperature.
examples: The manual refers to athermancy as a lost art.
Stories of athermancy often sound more like myths than facts.
Practitioners of athermancy were said to sense future events through warmth or cold.

senses.definition: Not allowing heat to pass through easily.
examples: The material is almost completely athermanous.
Designers choose athermanous layers to improve insulation.
An athermanous surface can reduce heat loss.

senses.definition: Not producing or involving a noticeable change in temperature.
examples: The treatment is athermic and does not heat the tissue.
Athermic processes are preferred to avoid thermal damage.
This is an athermic reaction with almost no heat exchange.

senses.definition: Not allowing heat to pass through; a variant of athermanous.
examples: An athermous barrier separated the hot and cold chambers.
The glass is treated to be almost completely athermous.
Athermous walls help maintain a stable temperature inside.

senses.definition: Plural of atherogenesis, the processes by which fatty deposits form in artery walls.
examples: Different atherogeneses may involve different risk factors.
Researchers compare atherogeneses in various animal models.
The article discusses possible atherogeneses in diabetic patients.

senses.definition: The process by which fatty deposits called atheromas develop in the walls of arteries.
examples: Smoking is known to speed up atherogenesis.
Scientists study atherogenesis to understand heart disease better.
Many factors contribute to atherogenesis over a person’s lifetime.

senses.definition: Tending to cause or promote the formation of fatty deposits in arteries.
examples: A high‑fat diet can be strongly atherogenic.
Researchers classify the substance as mildly atherogenic.
Atherogenic factors include smoking and high blood pressure.

senses.definition: A fatty deposit that builds up on the inside wall of an artery.
examples: An atheroma was found in one of the coronary arteries.
Large atheroma can reduce blood flow to the heart.
Ultrasound showed a small atheroma in the carotid artery.

senses.definition: Plural of atheroma, fatty deposits on the inner walls of arteries.
examples: Multiple atheromas were detected during the scan.
The surgeon removed several atheromas blocking the vessel.
A diet high in saturated fat can lead to more atheromas.

senses.definition: A dated medical term for fatty degeneration of the inner layer of arteries.
examples: Older medical books sometimes mention atheromasia of the aorta.
Atheromasia was once used to describe what we now call atherosclerosis.
The autopsy report referred to advanced atheromasia.

senses.definition: Plural of atheroma, especially in older or more technical writing.
examples: Several atheromata were present in the coronary arteries.
Large atheromata can seriously narrow the vessel.
The slide shows multiple atheromata in cross‑section.

senses.definition: A condition in which many atheromas are present in the arteries.
examples: The patient showed widespread atheromatosis of the major vessels.
Severe atheromatosis increases the risk of stroke.
Atheromatosis is common in older adults with long‑term risk factors.

senses.definition: Containing or relating to fatty deposits in the walls of arteries.
examples: An atheromatous plaque was identified in the carotid artery.
The aorta showed atheromatous changes throughout.
Atheromatous vessels are more likely to become blocked.

senses.definition: Plural of atherosclerosis, forms of artery disease involving fatty deposits in the walls.
examples: Different atheroscleroses can affect different parts of the body.
Researchers classify several atheroscleroses with distinct causes.
The paper compares atheroscleroses in various populations.

senses.definition: A disease in which fatty material builds up inside the arteries, making them narrow and hard.
examples: Atherosclerosis is a major cause of heart attacks and strokes.
Smoking greatly increases the risk of atherosclerosis.
Regular exercise can help prevent atherosclerosis.

senses.definition: Related to or affected by atherosclerosis.
examples: An atherosclerotic artery is narrowed by fatty deposits.
They studied atherosclerotic changes in the carotid vessels.
Atherosclerotic plaques can suddenly break and cause a clot.

senses.definition: In a way that involves or is caused by atherosclerotic changes in the arteries.
examples: The vessel is atherosclerotically narrowed.
The brain tissue was damaged atherosclerotically rather than by bleeding.
The heart failed atherosclerotically over many years.

senses.definition: A genus of evergreen trees from Australia, including the species commonly known as southern sassafras.
examples: Atherosperma grows naturally in cool, moist forests.
The wood of Atherosperma is light and pleasantly scented.
Atherosperma has been studied for its essential oils.

senses.definition: A genus of rodents in Asia known as brush‑tailed porcupines.
examples: Atherurus species are found in forested regions.
Hunters sometimes catch Atherurus for meat.
The zoo recently acquired a pair of Atherurus.

senses.definition: Plural of athetesis, critical rejections of words or passages as not genuine in a text.
examples: Scholars proposed several atheteses in the new edition.
The commentary lists the atheteses of earlier editors.
Their atheteses were not accepted by the wider community.

senses.definition: A decision by a scholar or editor that a word or passage in a text is not genuine and should be rejected.
examples: The editor’s athetesis of this line has been debated for years.
Athetesis is sometimes necessary when the manuscript is clearly corrupted.
Her athetesis removed several doubtful verses from the poem.

senses.definition: To mark or treat a word or passage in a text as not genuine and to reject it.
examples: The editor decided to atheticize the doubtful paragraph.
Scholars atheticize lines that they consider later additions.
Some critics refuse to atheticize any part of the play.

senses.definition: British spelling of athetize: to mark or treat a part of a text as not genuine and reject it.
examples: The editor chose to athetise several lines of the poem.
Some scholars athetise passages they view as later insertions.
It is risky to athetise text without strong evidence.

senses.definition: Marked or treated as not genuine in a text and therefore rejected, especially in scholarly editions.
examples: The athetised verses are printed in brackets.
Only a few words in the manuscript are athetised.
The editor’s athetised passages have been widely discussed.

senses.definition: Third person singular of athetise: marks or treats part of a text as not genuine and rejects it.
examples: When he athetises a passage, he explains his reasons in the notes.
The editor athetises anything that conflicts with the main manuscript.
If she athetises a line, she also suggests an alternative reading.

senses.definition: Present participle of athetise: marking or treating parts of a text as not genuine and rejecting them.
examples: The editor spent months athetising doubtful passages.
Athetising entire sections can seriously change the poem.
They are cautious about athetising lines without strong evidence.

senses.definition: To mark or treat a word or passage in a text as not genuine and reject it, especially in scholarly editing.
examples: The critic chose to athetize the final stanza.
Editors athetize lines that they believe were added later.
Some scholars are hesitant to athetize any part of the original text.

senses.definition: Marked or treated as not genuine and rejected in an edited text.
examples: The athetized lines are printed in the notes only.
An athetized passage is signaled by a special symbol.
Most of the athetized material comes from a later copy.
